story: A child (子) under a roof (宀) surrounded by knowledge (X) — the classic image of studying. Think of a child sheltered in a schoolhouse, absorbing wisdom from all sides.
A person (亻) leaning against a tree (木). Imagine a tired traveler who finally reaches a tree and rests against its trunk. When you see this kanji, picture yourself taking a break in the shade.
A person (亻) carrying something on their back — they can carry (可) anything. The question 'what?' is asking what a person is carrying. Think: 'What is that person holding?'
A cow (牛) walking on legs (儿) — going ahead of the herd. The cow takes the lead, moving forward on its legs. 'Ahead' of the rest.
Two mountains (山) stacked on top of each other. When one mountain rises out of another, something is 'coming out.' Think of a plant sprouting out of the ground.
Evening (夕) and mouth (口) — at dusk, you call someone's name with your mouth to find them in the dark. Your name is what people call out when they cannot see you.
A person (人) standing with arms stretched wide. The bigger something is, the more space it occupies. Picture a person spreading their arms to show 'this big!'
One (一) above big (大). Heaven is what lies one step above everything great. The sky stretches above all things on earth.
A woman kneeling with folded hands — a pictograph of a person in a traditional posture of grace. The curved lines suggest elegance and poise.
A baby wrapped in swaddling clothes with a large head — the classic shape of an infant. The round top is the head, the body is tucked below.
Three small drops or dots — tiny particles. When you see small things, they look like little specks. The kanji itself is small in size compared to 大 (big).
A pictograph of three mountain peaks. The center peak rises highest, flanked by two smaller ones. A literal drawing of a mountain range.
Three strokes representing the flow of a river. The left and right strokes are the banks, and the middle stroke is the water flowing between them.
A circle with a dot in the center — the ancient pictograph of the sun. The outer line is the sun's shape, and the dot may represent a sunspot or the center of daylight.
A crescent moon shape. Unlike the round sun (日), the moon appears in phases, and this kanji captures its crescent form. Two horizontal lines suggest the moon's surface features.
A pictograph of a tree with branches (top), trunk (middle), and roots (bottom). The vertical line is the trunk, the diagonal strokes are branches reaching up and roots going down.
A tree (木) with a mark (一) at its root. The mark indicates the 'origin' or 'base' of the tree. From this meaning, it came to mean 'book' — the source of knowledge.
A person standing with legs apart — a simple pictograph of a human figure. The two strokes represent legs, showing a person in profile.
A roof (人) covering dirt (土) with two dots (..) — gold nuggets hidden in the earth. The kanji suggests wealth buried underground, waiting to be uncovered.
A flowing stream with splashes on both sides — a pictograph of water in motion. The center line is the main current, and the side strokes are droplets splashing outward.
A person with arms raised, surrounded by flames on both sides. The shape evokes a campfire with flickering flames reaching upward.
Ground level (一) with a plant sprouting (十) from it. The cross shape represents a plant pushing up through the soil. Everything grows from the earth.
A person carrying a harvest of grain on their back. The passing of a year is marked by the cycle of planting and harvesting. Each harvest marks one year.
The sun (日) moving across the earth (土) while a person measures with a hand (寸). The sun's position at the temple tells you the time.
A gate (門) with sunlight (日) shining through the opening. The space 'between' the gate doors lets the light pass. It represents the interval or space between things.
Sheep (䒑) standing on legs (月) with a knife (刂) cutting in front. The sheep are led from the front, going before the shepherd.
A road (彳) with a thread (糸) and a reaching hand (夂) — what follows behind, like a thread trailing after weaving. What comes 'after' is connected to what came before like thread on a spool.
A short line above a longer line — something positioned 'above' a baseline. A simple and elegant representation of relative position.
A short line below a longer line — the opposite of 上. Something positioned 'below' the baseline. Together with 上, they form the basic vertical relationship.
A rectangle with a line passing through its center — an arrow piercing the middle of a target. The line through the box represents being 'inside' or at the 'center.'
Evening (夕) and divination (卜) — in ancient times, people performed divination rituals at dusk to learn about the world 'outside' their village. The 'outside' is unknown, requiring fortune-telling.
A hand (又) over a mouth (口) — most people use their right hand to bring food to their mouth. The right hand is the dominant hand for eating.
A hand (𠂇) helping at a workbench (工) — your left hand assists your dominant right hand during skilled work. The left hand supports.
The sun (日) rising behind a tree (木). The sun comes up from the east, appearing through the branches of a tree. The east is where the day begins.
A bird returning to its nest on a tree. The sun sets in the west, and birds fly home to roost at the end of the day. West is where the sun goes to rest.
A tree (木) inside a boundary (冂) facing the sun. In the northern hemisphere, the south side of a tree gets the most sunlight. The south is where the sun shines strongest.
Two people (匕) sitting back to back. When you face south (the sun), your back is to the north. The north is behind you, the direction of shadow.
One (一) and a white (白) sun marking the number. A hundred is a 'white' or clear milestone. Think of a white page with a single bold mark — one hundred.
A person (亻) with a line (十) through them — ten hundreds make a thousand. The cross mark emphasizes the multiplier effect.
Originally a swastika-like symbol representing infinity or the number 10,000. It curved into the modern shape representing an endless number — 'myriads' of things.
A round shape with a line through it — a pictograph of a coin. The circle represents the coin's shape, and the line shows its value. The yen is Japan's currency.
A roof (一) with water droplets (丶) falling from the sky. The outer line is the cloud cover, and the dots are raindrops falling downward. A perfect pictograph of rainfall.
Rain (雨) hitting a field (田) with a lightning bolt (乚) striking. Electricity arcs through the sky like lightning during a storm, connecting the clouds to the ground.
Steam (气) rising from rice (米) — the life energy or spirit that animates all living things. Ki (気) is the invisible energy that flows through everything.
Words (言) spoken in a group of a thousand (千) tongues plus the tongue radical (舌). Language and speech involve many tongues sharing words.
Words (言) spoken by five (五) mouths (口) — language involves many people speaking together. A language requires multiple speakers to survive.
Words (言) for sale (売) — when you read a book, you consume words that someone has 'sold' through writing. Reading is purchasing ideas with your attention.
A hand holding a brush (聿) writing on a surface (曰). The ancient art of calligraphy — using a brush dipped in ink to create characters on paper.
An eye (目) on legs (儿) — a walking eye that goes places to see things. To see is to actively direct your eyes toward something.
An ear (耳) at the gate (門) — you put your ear to the gate to listen for news from outside. Hearing is the ear receiving information through the doorway.
A roof (人) over good (良) rice — good food under a roof, prepared for eating. The kanji depicts a meal: food that nourishes and satisfies.
A person bending over a container (欠) to drink from a food vessel (食). Thirst makes you lean forward to drink. Picture someone sipping soup from a bowl.
An intersection (彳) with steps (亍) — moving forward step by step along a path. To 'go' is to put one foot in front of the other on your journey.
Wheat (来) growing toward you — the grain 'comes' from the field to your table. When something comes, it approaches from a distance like a harvest arriving.
A wedge or arrowhead pointing inward. Unlike 人 (person), which has legs apart, 入 has strokes that converge inward like something entering a space.
A person (大) standing on the ground (一) — standing firmly on the earth. The horizontal line at the bottom represents the ground, and above it a person stands tall.
A sprout (𠂉) emerging from the earth (土). Life pushes up from the soil as a seedling reaches for the sun. All life begins with growth from the ground.
Two hands (又) joining together in friendship. A handshake or clasping hands — the universal gesture of friendship and companionship.
A tree (木) where learning is exchanged (交) — in ancient times, students gathered under trees to learn from their teachers. School is where knowledge is shared.
A woman with two breasts, depicted from the front. The two dots represent nurturing breasts, and the enclosing lines suggest a mother's embrace. A mother nourishes and protects.
A hand holding an axe or tool — the father as the worker and provider. In ancient times, a father's role involved wielding tools to provide for the family.
A field (田) with power (力) — in traditional society, men worked the fields with their strength. The kanji associates masculinity with agricultural labor and physical power.
